*[Bug 19071] wilcox.test and precision when detecting zeros*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19071>

Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
         Resolution|---                         |FIXED
             Status|REOPENED                    |CLOSED

--- Comment #9 from Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) ---
I have now committed svn rev 90068 to R-devel, addressing 
- this bug, introducing a `digits.zap` argument and using it in a __robust__
version of zapsmall, see above
- *and* bug 18035 (lowering `digits.rank`'s default).

Even though addressing and fixing too bugs, I suggest to keep this in R-devel
for now,
as it *does* change results (rarely, and of course in the case we'd *want* a
change) which for such a fundamental test (family) as `wilcox.test()` may
should not happen lightly.

*[Bug 18035] a bug in wilcox.test() caused by precision error when there are ties*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18035>

Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
             Status|ASSIGNED                    |CLOSED
         Resolution|---                         |FIXED

--- Comment #8 from Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) ---
As just said in Bug's 19071 Comment 9, I've committed a change to R-devel which
sets the default value of `digits.rank` to `7`  (instead of `Inf`).

With particular thanks to Michael Chirico's exploration and findings in comment
6.

*[Bug 18364] Initializing Column Improper Length*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18364>

Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
             Status|TRIAGED                     |CLOSED
         Resolution|---                         |FIXED

--- Comment #8 from Martin Maechler (<mailto:maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch|maechler@stat.math.ethz.ch>) ---
(In reply to Gabriel Becker from comment #5)
> Created attachment 3700
> Doc patch for Extract.data.frame

Thank you, Gabe.  Note that we (R core) had decided to use _data frame_ as
English word (and `data.frame` as part of the R language).

I've committed +/- Gabe's patch, now (svn r90069).  
Further notes:
 I've replaced _non-assigned-to_  by something Kurt would not have to wrap by
`\I{...}`  ;-)
- The link `\manual{R-lang}{Subset-assignment}` unfortunately currently does
*not* produce the correct
<https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-lang.html#Subset-assignment>
 but rather mangles the hyphen (`-`).

I think this is bug in our `Rd2html()` but leave this for others to diagnose.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

--- Comment #9 from Sebastian Meyer (<mailto:seb.meyer@fau.de|seb.meyer@fau.de>) ---
(In reply to Martin Maechler from comment #8)
This is correct. The syntax is as exemplified in `share/Rd/macros/system.Rd`,

    % To cite an R manual, e.g.: \manual{R-exts}{Processing documentation
files}

i.e., the second argument is a node name (usually a section title). So the fix
amounts to replacing `{Subset-assignment}` by `{Subset assignment}`. Committed
in r90070.

text:
*[Bug 18874] Nested grid viewports with null produce incorrect grob rendering on small windows*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18874>

Trevor Davis (<mailto:trevor.l.davis@gmail.com|trevor.l.davis@gmail.com>) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |trevor.l.davis@gmail.com

--- Comment #1 from Trevor Davis (<mailto:trevor.l.davis@gmail.com|trevor.l.davis@gmail.com>) ---
> For smaller sizes, the line incorrectly flips downward or disappears entirely.

In the `X11()` device on Linux I can reproduce the line flipping downwards but
can't replicate it disappearing (instead when the `linesGrob()` gets a
zero-height viewport I observe a horizontal line).

In the example after converting the 1 inch to 2.54 cm we ultimately have the
following layout:

1. 5 cm row
2. 1 null row (nested with 3. in a "null" row) with a `linesGrob()` in it
3. 2.54 cm row (nested with 2. in a "null" row)
4. 5 cm row

We note that this layout explicitly reserves 12.54 cm of height for whitespace
and gives the rest of the height to the `linesGrob()` row.

Observations when running example in X11() in R-devel on Linux:

1.  When the device is 12.54 cm and higher the `linesGrob()` exactly gets the
rest of the height (as expected) eventually getting assigned to a viewport with
a height of 0 cm when the device is exactly 12.54 cm.
2.  When the device is between 10 cm and 12.54 cm tall the `linesGrob()` still
gets assigned a height of 0 cm and remains a horizontal line.
3. When the device is less than 10 cm the `linesGrob()` gets assigned a
viewport with a height of **negative** 2.54 centimeters which also flips the
direction of the `linesGrob()` so it has an unexpected negative slope and fixes
its height when the overall height is less than 10 cm.


The behaviour in 3. might possibly be a bug in `allocationRemaining()` in
`src/library/grid/src/layout.c`.  The middle "null" row get's assigned a height
of zero so `allocationRemaining()` gets passed an `initial = 0` (since the
viewport `parentHeightCM` is 0) and a `remaining = -2.54` (since the 1 inch row
takes 2.54 centimeters) which eventually leads to that nested "null" row
getting assigned a height of **negative** 2.54 centimeters.  Instead of 

```
bool allocationRemaining(double initial, double remaining)
{
    if (initial == 0) {
        return TRUE;
    } else if (initial > 0) {
        return remaining > 0;
    } else {
        return remaining < 0;
    }
}
```

I speculate we may want something like

```
bool allocationRemaining(double initial, double remaining)
{
    if (initial >= 0) {
        return remaining > 0;
    } else {
        return remaining < 0;
    }
}
```

which should lead to this "null" row getting zeroed out even when the total
height is less than 10 cm.

Or alternatively this may be a situation we may want to throw a clear error
(i.e. the layout requires 12.54 cm of height and it isn't be given its required
height)?

I'm not entirely clear when/if "null" rows/columns should ever be assigned a
negative height/width but `allocationRemaining()` implies that if the "null" is
in a negative length viewport and the remaining part of the negative length
viewport is negative then the "null" viewport should also have a negative
length?  I skimmed Murrell's paper referenced in `?grid::grid.layout` and his
book `R Graphics` (vol 2) doesn't seem to mention when we should allow or
disallow negative length viewport dimensions and how they should behave...

elements.text: [Bug 18874] Nested grid viewports with null produce incorrect grob rendering on small windows

) changed:

           What    |Removed                     |Added
----------------------------------------------------------------------------
                 CC|                            |trevor.l.davis@gmail.com

--- Comment #10 from Suharto Anggono (<mailto:suharto_anggono@yahoo.com|suharto_anggono@yahoo.com>) ---
(In reply to Gabriel Becker from comment #5)
> Created attachment 3700
> Doc patch for Extract.data.frame

In the committed one in r90069, "in conjunction" is removed. As documented in
the quoted part in Comment 2, `x[i, j] <- value` can add rows.

Also, modified from
<https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72618429/why-does-r-allow-new-column-creation-via-tail-slicing/72619279#72619279>
, which "does not works" is not quite true:

    df = data.frame(a = 1:4, b = 5:8)
    df$z1[1] <- 1 # recycles 1 element
    df$z2[2] <- 1 # recycles 2 elements
    df$z3[3] <- 1 # error

.

text:
*[Bug 19076] New: quartz() bitmap devices (png/jpeg/tiff/bmp) ignore 'res' in output file metadata*
<https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=19076>

            Bug ID: 19076
           Summary: quartz() bitmap devices (png/jpeg/tiff/bmp) ignore
                    'res' in output file metadata
           Product: R
           Version: R 4.6.x
          Hardware: All
                OS: macOS
            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: minor
          Priority: P5
         Component: Graphics
          Assignee: <mailto:R-core@R-project.org|R-core@R-project.org>
          Reporter: <mailto:bill.r-project@denney.ws|bill.r-project@denney.ws>

Created attachment 3707
  --> <https://bugs.r-project.org/attachment.cgi?id=3707&action=edit>
Possible patch for Quartz to honor dpi

On macOS, when png(), jpeg(), tiff(), or bmp() is called with
type = "quartz" (the default value of getOption("bitmapType") on
macOS builds without cairo), the 'res' argument is used to choose
the pixel dimensions of the bitmap but is NOT written into the
output file as the resolution tag.  Programs that read the file
back (Preview, sips, exiftool, Pillow, ImageMagick, etc.) report
the file as 72 DPI regardless of the requested value.

The cairo back-end (cairo/cairoBM.c), the Xlib back-end
(modules/X11/devX11.c) and the Windows native back-end
(grDevices/src/devWindows.c) all already pass the requested
resolution to R_SaveAsPng / R_SaveAsJpeg / R_SaveAsTIFF /
R_SaveAsBmp in grDevices/src/winbitmap.c, which write the
appropriate tag (pHYs for PNG, JFIF density for JPEG,
XRESOLUTION/YRESOLUTION/RESOLUTIONUNIT for TIFF, XPels/YPels per
metre for BMP).

The Quartz back-end does not.  QuartzBitmap_Output() in
src/library/grDevices/src/qdBitmap.c calls

    CGImageDestinationAddImage(dest, image, NULL);

passing NULL for the properties dictionary, so ImageIO writes
whatever DPI the destination type defaults to (72 for PNG/JPEG).

Reproducer on macOS:

    png("test.png", width = 4, height = 3, units = "in",
        res = 300, type = "quartz")
    plot(1:10)
    dev.off()
    system("sips -g dpiWidth -g dpiHeight test.png")
    ## reported: 72 / 72   (expected: 300 / 300)

The same test against type = "cairo" returns 300 / 300, as does
the same script run on Linux (cairo) and Windows (devga).

Patch attached (against trunk r90071, src/library/grDevices/src/
qdBitmap.c only).  It:

  * adds 'has_dpi' and 'dpi[2]' fields to QuartzBitmapDevice and
    populates them in QuartzBitmap_DeviceCreate using
    (par->dpi != NULL) as the "user supplied an explicit res"
    signal -- this is the same signal devQuartz.c::Quartz() uses
    when it builds the QuartzParameters_t (dpi = 0 when res = NA);

  * in QuartzBitmap_Output, builds a CFDictionary with
    kCGImagePropertyDPIWidth / kCGImagePropertyDPIHeight when
    has_dpi is set, and passes it to both
    CGImageDestinationAddImage() call sites (file and clipboard);

  * only writes the tag when res was explicitly given, so res = NA
    continues to leave the format default in place -- matching the
    behaviour of the cairo, Xlib and devga back-ends, which all
    guard the tag with "if (res > 0)".

I have verified that the cairo back-end correctly embeds 'res' for
all four formats at 72/96/150/300/600 DPI on Linux (using Pillow
and a direct struct-level parse of the pHYs/JFIF/TIFF/BMP headers
as the external readers).  I do not have a macOS box to test the
Quartz fix empirically; review on that platform would be
appreciated.

            Status: UNCONFIRMED
          Severity: minor
          Priority: P5
         Component: Graphics
          Assignee:

Possible patch for Quartz to honor dpi

On macOS, when png(), jpeg(), tiff(), or bmp() is called with

appreciated.
